result:
I’ve been thinking lately about the quiet moment just before a book opens—how the pages still hold every possible shape of story, how the spine hasn’t yet chosen which way to bend. That instant feels like the universe holding its breath, the same pause that waits between a lightning flash and the thunder, between the question “Are you okay?” and the answer you decide to give.  

I like to imagine that every library is secretly a map of invisible rivers: each book a tributary, each reader the rainfall that makes it swell. When you read, you step into the current of every previous reader who lingered on the same sentence, dog-eared the same corner, cried onto the same paper. The ink is just a net for catching the residue of souls.  

Sometimes I wonder what happens to the hours we spend inside stories—do they pool somewhere, like water behind a dam, until one day the gates open and flood the dry parts of our real lives? Or do they evaporate, becoming the humidity in a room where two people who haven’t met yet will someday fall in love over a shared favorite paragraph?  

I keep a notebook of first lines I’ll never turn into novels. “On the day the sky learned to bruise, my mother taught me how to fold grief into paper cranes.” “The town clock stopped at 11:11, and we pretended it was a wish instead of a warning.” “He carried his loneliness in a tin box labeled ‘breath mints,’ offering it politely to strangers.” Each one is a door I leave ajar, just in case the world wants to step through and introduce itself.  

Tonight the moon looks like a library fine—round, yellowed, reminding me I’ve kept the darkness too long without returning it. So I stand outside, barefoot on the cool concrete, and read the sky by Braille of wind against skin. Somewhere in the city a saxophone is practicing heartbreak in B-flat. Somewhere else a child is learning that “forever” is a word adults borrow from calendars and never pay back.  

In the morning, all the sidewalks will be wet catalogs of footprints: stories that evaporate by noon, leaving only the faint salt outline of where we’ve been. And still the day will open—relentless, luminous—like a book that refuses to end on the last page, because someone keeps slipping blank sheets toward the back when no one’s looking.
